coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes in the measurement of the critical micelle concentration of surfactants
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Dongxiang
Qian, Xing
Lian, Changshuo
Huang, Rui
Hao, Ziyi
Cheng, Ming
Li, Shuping
Li, Chunfang
Iqbal, Shahid
Qin, Lianjie
Zhao, Jikuan
coredata.dc:description: The critical micelle concentration (CMC) is one main parameter of surfactants, so an accurate, rapid, and convenient method for CMC determination is of great significance for surfactant applications. In this paper, three samples of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with different fluorescence properties were developed as fluorescent probes for the CMC determination of CTAB, SDS, Triton X-100, perfluoro OBS and Pluronics® P-123. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of a constant amount of the CQDs in different concentrations of the surfactant solution were recorded, and then the CMC values of the surfactant were deduced from the fluorescence intensity change and the Stokes shift variation. The obtained CMC data using three samples of the CQDs agree well with those in the published literature. It is also demonstrated that in the micellization process the interaction between the CQDs and the surfactant molecules is complicated, because the fluorescence properties of the CQDs are found to be related to the characteristics of the CQDs, the head group type of ionic surfactants, the chemical structure between the hydrophobic and hydrophilic segment, and the chemical nature of the hydrophobic chains of the surfactants. So, the CQDs are demonstrated to be located at the palisade layer in the surfactant micelles due to the surface-attached polar groups on the CQDs although the CQDs are mainly hydrophobic as fluorescent probes. The fluorescent CQDs are considered to be new choices of the fluorescent probes in the facile CMC measurement of the surfactants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent probes
Surfactant
Critical micelle concentration

CMC determination by fluorescence intensity changes
CMC determination by Stokes shift variations
Comparative analysis of the CMC data

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optical photos of CQDs under daylight (A) and 365 nm UV light (B), and 3D fluorescence spectra of G-CQDs (C), Y-CQDs (D) and O-CQDs (E).
TEM images of G-CQDs (A), Y-CQDs (B), O-CQDs (C), and high-resolution TEM image of O-CQDs (D).
(A) UV-vis absorption spectra of CQDs, (B) FT-IR spectra of the CQDs, (C) Raman spectra of CQDs (785 nm laser as excitation source).

Fluorescence emission spectra (A, B, C) and the corresponding fluorescence intensity data (D, E, F) of the mixture solutions of G-CQDs (A, D), Y-CQDs (B, E) and O-CQDs (C, F) as fluorescent probes and CTAB with different concentration.

Fluorescence emission spectra (A, B, C) and the corresponding fluorescence intensity data (D, E, F) of the mixture solutions of G-CQDs (A, D), Y-CQDs (B, E) and O-CQDs (C, F) as fluorescent probes and different concentration of SDS.

Fluorescence emission spectra (A, B, C) and the corresponding fluorescence intensity data (D, E, F) of the mixture solutions of G-CQDs (A, D), Y-CQDs (B, E) and O-CQDs (C, F) as fluorescent probes and different concentration of Triton X-100.

Fluorescence emission spectra (A, B, C) and the corresponding fluorescence intensity data (D, E, F) of the mixture solutions of G-CQDs (A, D), Y-CQDs (B, E) and O-CQDs (C, F) as fluorescent probes and perfluoro surfactant OBS with different concentration.

Fluorescence emission spectra (A, B, C) and the fluorescence intensity data (D, E, F) of the mixture solutions of G-CQDs (A, D), Y-CQDs (B, E) and O-CQDs (C, F) as fluorescent probes and a triblock copolymer surfactant P-123 of varied concentration.

The Stokes shift of the mixture of CTAB (A, B, C), SDS (D, E, F), or Triton X-100 (G, H, I) at different concentrations and G-CQDs (A, D, G), Y-CQDs (B, E, H), or O-CQDs (C, F, I) as fluorescent probes, respectively.
The Stokes shift of the mixture of OBS (A, B, C) or P-123 (D, E, F) at different concentration and G-CQDs (A, D), Y-CQDs (B, E) or O-CQDs (C, F) as fluorescent probes, respectively.
The CMC value of surfactants as obtained from our studies compared with literature values.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The surfactant in the literature is an isomer of the one herein.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The CMC value of P-123 is strongly dependent on temperature, so the data were measured at accurate 20 ℃ for a comparion with the reference data.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes in the measurement of the critical micelle concentration of surfactants

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Lab of Colloids and Functional Nanostructures, College of Chemistry and Molecular Engineering, Qingdao University of Science and Technology (QUST)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The critical micelle concentration (CMC) is one main parameter of surfactants, so an accurate, rapid, and convenient method for CMC determination is of great significance for surfactant applications. In this paper, three samples of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with different fluorescence properties were developed as fluorescent probes for the CMC determination of CTAB, SDS, Triton X-100, perfluoro OBS and Pluronics® P-123. The fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of a constant amount of the CQDs in different concentrations of the surfactant solution were recorded, and then the CMC values of the surfactant were deduced from the fluorescence intensity change and the Stokes shift variation. The obtained CMC data using three samples of the CQDs agree well with those in the published literature. It is also demonstrated that in the micellization process the interaction between the CQDs and the surfactant molecules is complicated, because the fluorescence properties of the CQDs are found to be related to the characteristics of the CQDs, the head group type of ionic surfactants, the chemical structure between the hydrophobic and hydrophilic segment, and the chemical nature of the hydrophobic chains of the surfactants. So, the CQDs are demonstrated to be located at the palisade layer in the surfactant micelles due to the surface-attached polar groups on the CQDs although the CQDs are mainly hydrophobic as fluorescent probes. The fluorescent CQDs are considered to be new choices of the fluorescent probes in the facile CMC measurement of the surfactants.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with different fluorescence properties were developed as fluorescent probes for the CMC determination of typical surfactants based on the fluorescence intensity change and the Stokes shift variation. The obtained CMC data using three samples of the CQDs agree well with those in the published literature. In the micellization process the interaction between the CQDs and the surfactant molecules is complicated, and the CQDs are speculated to be located at the palisade layer in the surfactant micelles.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Surfactants have displayed great interest due to their outstanding advantages such as simplicity, practicality, chemical stability, selectivity, and cost-effectiveness [1,2], so they are extensively studied in the fields of petrochemicals [3–5], corrosion protection [6,7], food [8,9], environmental management [10,11] and biomedical applications [12,13], as well as the size-controlled production of nanoparticles and catalytic applications [14–21]. The critical micelle concentration (CMC) is one of the main parameters of the surfactants and therefore the accurate, rapid, and easy determination of the CMC is of great importance for the surfactant applications.

To our knowledge, based on the sharp variation of physicochemical properties near the CMC, many methods including the surface tension method [22,23], conductometry [22–24], light scattering [25,26] and fluorescence methods [22, 27–29] have been developed to determine the CMC. However, the surface tension method is tedious, time-consuming, and sensitive to environmental temperature. Conductometry is only suitable for ionic surfactants and light scattering is less sensitive. Whereas, the fluorescence methods show the advantages of simple operation and high sensitivity [30], in which fluorescent probes such as pyrene [31,32], coumarin [28] and aggregation-induced luminescent molecules [33–35] are usually employed to determine the CMC. However, the virulent, poor water-soluble and costly nature of the organic fluorescent probes limits their broad application. Thus, it is necessary to develop novel environmental-friendly, highly sensitive and price-effective fluorescent probes for the determination of the CMC.

In the past years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted increasing attention due to their outstanding advantages such as good chemical stability, environmental friendliness, luminescence stability, low toxicity and low cost [36–44], and they are extensively studied in the fields of light-emitting materials [45–47], sensors [48–50], food safety [50–52], bio-imaging [53,54] and fluorescent probes [39,55,56]. At present, the CQDs as fluorescent probes are widely used in the selective detection of public safety substances, organic amines and metal ions [39,56,57]. In our previous work, the blue-emitting CQDs were found to be readily phase-transferred into various organic solvents and showed enhanced fluorescence [58], so they are thought to be easily entrapped into the surfactant micelles, and such environment change around the CQDs will affect the fluorescence properties of the CQDs. Therefore, the CQDs can be expected as potential fluorescent probes in the measurement of the CMC.

Recently, ratiometric fluorescent probes were developed to determine the CMC of the non-ionic and ionic surfactants based on gel-column purified dual-emissive carbon dots [59]. In this work, three samples of the CQDs were synthesized from L-cystine and o-phenylenediamine by a microwave hydrothermal method [58, 60–62] and the as-obtained CQDs were directly employed as fluorescent probes to determine the CMC of representative surfactants including cationic cetyltrimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB), anionic sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), non-ionic Triton X-100, sodium perfluorononyloxybenzenesulfonate (OBS) and triblock copolymer Pluronics® P-123. Based on the fluorescence properties of certain amount CQDs in different concentration of the surfactant solutions, the CMC values can be deduced from the fluorescence intensity changes and the Stokes shift variation. The obtained CMC data by the three samples of the CQDs agree well with those in the published literatures. So, the CQDs as facile fluorescent probes show a significant potential in the measurement the CMC of surfactants.

CTAB and Pluronics® P-123 (EO20-b-PO70-b-EO20, Mn ∼5800) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). L-cystine was purchased from Macklin Reagent (China). Sodium perfluorononyloxybenzenesulfonate (OBS, CAS NO. 77061–68–8) was purchased from Adamas-beta Reagent (China), and o-phenylenediamine was purchased from Aladdin Reagent (China). Sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), Triton X-100, sodium hydroxide and other solvents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent (China). All reagents were used directly without further purification, and the ultrapure deionized water was used in the experiments.

Three samples of the CQDs were synthesized by a single-mode microwave reactor (NOVA-2S, PreeKem Scientific, China). First, the green fluorescence CQDs (G-CQDs) were synthesized from a precursor L-cystine [58]. L-cystine (1.0 g) was dissolved in 10 mL NaOH solution (0.95 mol/L), followed by sonication to make the mixture clear in a sealed high-pressure-resistant container. The reaction was conducted for 5–10 s at 135 °C in the microwave reactor. The obtained sample was pale yellow and glowed green fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (named as G-CQDs). Second, yellow and orange fluorescence CQDs (Y-CQDs and O-CQDs) were prepared from a precursor o-phenylenediamine via modified conditions according to the references [61,62]. O-phenylenediamine (0.2 g) was dissolved in 10 mL water and sonicated to be clear in the container, then the reaction was conducted for 5 min at 135 °C in the reactor, and the obtained yellow solution showed shone yellow fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (Y-CQDs). Moreover, o-phenylenediamine (0.1 g) was dissolved in 10 mL NaOH solution (0.95 mol/L) and sonicated to be clear, then the solution was heated to 135 °C for 5 min in the reactor. The obtained brownish-yellow solution showed orange fluorescence under a 365 nm UV lamp (O-CQDs).

A series of aqueous solutions of CTAB, SDS, Triton X-100, OBS or P-123 were prepared with different concentrations in a total volume of 4.0 mL, which contained a same 50 μL aqueous solution of the as-prepared CQDs. According to the CMC values in published literature, the surfactant concentration for CTAB and OBS ranged from 0.20 mM to 2.0 mM with 0.20 mM intervals. As for SDS, the concentration ranged from 2.0 mM to 20 mM with 2.0 mM intervals for the G-CQDs and the O-CQDs as fluorescent probes, while they ranged from 1.0 mM to 10 mM with 1.0 mM intervals for the Y-CQDs as fluorescent probes. Moreover, the surfactant concentration range was from 0.10 mM to 0.30 mM with 0.02 mM intervals for Triton X-100, while it was from 0.05 mM to 0.50 mM with 0.05 mM intervals for P-123.

All solutions were sonicated for 5 min to achieve equilibrium of the interaction between the CQDs and the surfactant molecules; thereafter the excitation and emission spectra were recorded using the fluorescence spectrometer. Then, the CMC value of the surfactant was determined from the graphical representation of the fluorescence emission intensity versus surfactant concentration. Furthermore, the Stokes shift was calculated from the emission maxima and the excitation maxima, and then the CMC value was deduced from the Stokes shift variation versus the surfactant concentration.

Fluorescence spectra and UV–vis spectra were collected on an F-2700 spectrometer (HITACHI, Japan) and a TU-1901 spectrometer (Beijing Purkinje General, China), respectively. Fourier Transform Infrared (FT-IR) spectra were obtained on a Nicolet IS10 IR spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, USA), while Raman spectra were recorded on a Invia Qontor spectrometer (Renishaw, UK) with a 785 nm laser source. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CQDs were obtained on a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of the CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The CQDs were synthesized by microwave-assisted hydrothermal carbonization from carbon sources, L-cystine and o-phenylenediamine [58, 60–62]. The obtained three samples of the CQDs show pale yellow, yellow and brownish colors under daylight ( Fig. 1A), and they emit green, yellow and orange fluorescence under the irradiation of 365 nm UV light (Fig. 1B), respectively. The 3-dimensional fluorescence spectra of three samples of the CQDs are shown in Fig. 1C-1E. The maximum excitation and maximum emission wavelengths are found to be 425 nm and 505 nm for the G-CQDs, respectively (Fig. 1C). Obviously, the maximum emission wavelength of the G-CQDs does not vary when the excitation wavelength ranges from 370 nm to 475 nm, showing an independent emission to the excitation [63]. Fig. 1D indicates two maximum excitation wavelengths at 375 nm and 470 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength at 556 nm for the Y-CQDs. Fig. 1E implies two maximum excitation wavelengths at 380 nm and 470 nm, and the maximum emission wavelength at 570 nm for the O-CQDs. The maximum emission wavelengths of both the Y-CQDs and the O-CQDs are independent to the excitation wavelength although the excitation wavelength has a broad range from 320 nm to 520 nm [63].

Fig. 2 shows TEM images of the three samples of the CQDs. It is obvious that the morphology of the CQDs is a spherical shape in a well-dispersed state. From Fig. 2A and the insetted size distribution diagram, the particle size of the G-CQDs ranges from 1 nm to 4 nm with an average of 2.53 nm, which is similar to that in the previous work [58]. The particle size of the Y-CQDs has a range of 1.5–4.5 nm (Fig. 2B) with an average of 3.13 nm, while that of the O-CQDs ranges from 1.6 nm to 4.0 nm with an average of 2.72 nm (Fig. 2C). The high-resolution TEM image of the O-CQDs in Fig. 2D shows a distinct crystal lattice. The spacing of the lattice fringe is calculated to be 0.214 nm, corresponding to the < 100 > facet of graphene [63,64].

Furthermore, UV–vis absorption spectra of the CQDs were shown in Fig. 3A. The G-CQDs show weak absorption above 400 nm but obvious absorption as below 380 nm, while there are typical absorption peaks of the Y-CQDs and the O-CQDs at 420 nm and 430 nm, respectively. Such UV–vis absorption spectra correspond to the sample color under daylight. As shown in Fig. 3B, FT-IR spectra were collected to identify the functional groups on the surface of the CQDs after purification by a phase-transfer into chloroform. For the G-CQDs, the absorption peak of the deformation vibration of -NH2 at 1591 cm−1 is very weak, which indicates the very little amino group on the dot surface. The weak absorption at 1730 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching vibration of -CO, which may imply the presence of aldehyde, ketone and carboxyl groups. The broad absorption peak at 3343 cm−1 should be ascribed to the hydroxyl groups on the surface of the G-CQDs. For the Y-CQDs and the O-CQDs, the double peaks at 3384 cm−1 and 3363 cm−1 indicate stretching vibrations of primary amine N-H, and double peaks at 1630 cm−1 and 1591 cm−1 are ascribed to the bending vibrations of primary amine N-H. The strong peaks might indicate a large amount of -NH2 on the surface of the Y-CQDs and the O-CQDs. The double broad peaks at 3289 cm−1 and 3190 cm−1 demonstrate the existence of -OH groups [62]. As shown in Fig. 3 C, the three samples of the CQDs have similar patterns of Raman spectra. The D-band at 1270 cm−1 corresponds to sp3 disordered carbon atom vibration, while the G-band at 1590 cm−1 corresponds to sp2 ordered graphitic carbon. The ratio of the peak intensity of the D-band and G-band (ID/IG) represents the disorder extent, implying the extent of structural defects [65]. The calculated ID/IG ratio is 1.42 for the G-CQDs, while it is 1.62 and 1.46 for the Y-CQDs and the O-CQDs, respectively. The ID/IG value of the Y-CQDs is obvious larger than others, which may indicate more functional groups on the surface of the Y-CQDs. As combined with the FT-IR analysis, more amino groups may exist on the surface of the Y-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CMC determination by fluorescence intensity changes
ce:section.ce:para: To our knowledge, surfactant molecules exist as a single molecule state at the concentration below CMC in water, while they will assemble/aggregate into micelles once the concentration reaches the CMC. The surfactant micelles can entrap the CQDs into their cores due to the relative lipophilicity of the CQDs, then the changed environment around the CQDs in the micelles will significantly affect the fluorescence properties of the CQDs.

Fig. 4A-C shows the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixture containing a same amount of the CQDs and different concentrations of cationic CTAB, the corresponding fluorescence intensity data versus the surfactant concentration are collected in Fig. 4D-F. The fluorescence intensity for any cases gradually increases as the CTAB concentration increases. This may be ascribed to the increasing environmental lipophilicity of the CQDs. The slope of the curves in Fig. 4D-F changes versus the surfactant concentration, and the inflection point is considered as the CMC value of the surfactant. So, the CMC values of CTAB are determined to be 0.97 mM, 0.74 mM, and 0.98 mM for the G-CQDs, Y-CQDs, and O-CQDs as fluorescent probes, respectively. These data are in good agreement with earlier published results [59,66]. It is also noted that the slope of the fitted lines at low concentration (< CMC) is larger than that at high concentration (> CMC) when the G-CQDs and the O-CQDs are used as fluorescent probes (Figs. 4D and 4F), whereas it is reverse as the Y-CQDs are used as fluorescent probes. This should be attributed to the difference of the surface characteristics of the CQDs, because the surface groups on the CQDs will affect hydrophilic-lipophilic ability and surface charge.

Fig. 5A-C shows the changes in the fluorescence emission spectra of the CQDs under different concentrations of anionic SDS and the fluorescence intensity data are collected in Fig. 5D-F. It is observed that the fluorescence intensity of the G-CQDs decreases first slowly and then falls rapidly as increasing the SDS concentration, so the inflection point of 6.13 mM can be considered as the CMC value (Fig. 5D). For the Y-CQDs as fluorescent probes, the fluorescence intensity first falls and then increases with the increasing of the SDS concentration; an inflection point of 6.88 mM can be recognized as the CMC (Fig. 5E). Differently, for O-CQDs as probes the fluorescence intensity increases rapidly at low concentrations and then slowly at high concentrations, an inflection point of 7.70 mM is considered as the CMC (Fig. 5F). Noticeably, for the G-CQDs and the Y-CQDs, the change tendency of the fluorescence intensity with SDS concentrations is different to that for CTAB. This difference should be related to the difference of the reverse charged ionic head group of SDS and CTAB. This phenomenon implies a short distance between the ionic head groups and the CQDs in the micelles and indicates that the CQDs possibly are located at the palisade layer of the surfactant micelles, not in the hydrophobic cores due to the attached hydrophilic groups on the CQDs. Nevertheless, the deduced CMC values by the three samples of CQDs are all agree well with the values measured by other methods [29,34].

The fluorescence spectra and the collected fluorescence intensity data of the mixture solutions of the CQDs and different concentrations of nonionic Triton X-100 are shown in Fig. 6. With increasing the concentration of Triton X-100, the fluorescence intensity increases rapidly and then increases slowly for the G-CQDs as fluorescent probes, but it increases rapidly and then decreases rapidly for the Y-CQDs, while it reduces rapidly first and then slowly for the O-CQDs. Obviously, the change tendency of the fluorescence intensity shows much difference, which demonstrates a complicated interaction between the CQDs and the surfactant micelles. The inflection point at 0.24 mM for the G-CQDs in Fig. 6D, 0.22 mM for the Y-CQDs in Fig. 6E, and 0.23 mM for the O-CQDs in Fig. 6F can be considered as the CMC values of Triton X-100, which is in accordance with those determined by other methods [34,67].

Similarly, Fig. 7 shows the fluorescence emission spectra (Fig. 7A-C) and the corresponding intensity data (Fig. 7D-F) of the mixtures of the CQDs and a perfluoro surfactant OBS with different concentrations. Though OBS is a perfluoro anionic surfactant, the change tendency of the fluorescence intensity for the three samples of the CQDs (Fig. 7D-F) is similar to that of the hydrocarbon anionic surfactant SDS (Fig. 5D-F). This verifies the interaction between the CQDs and the charged ionic head groups of the surfactant and the location at the palisade layer of the CQDs in the micelles. However, when the G-CQDs are used as fluorescent probes the slope of the fitted lines before and after micellization changes in different manners for SDS and OBS, this should be attributed to the different chemical nature of the hydrophobic chains. The deduced CMC value of OBS is 0.89 mM by the G-CQDs, 0.95 mM by the Y-CQDs and 0.80 mM by the O-CQDs, respectively. These values are basically in agreement with that of an isomer of OBS [68].

Furthermore, the fluorescence emission spectra of the mixtures of the CQDs and a triblock copolymer Pluronics® P-123 with varied concentration at 20 °C are shown in Fig. 8A-C, and the collected fluorescence intensity data are shown in Fig. 8D-F. The fluorescence intensity using the G-CQDs as fluorescent probes increases in the low concentration range and then decreases in the high concentration range (Fig. 8D); the inflection point of 0.24 mM is looked as the CMC for P-123. For the Y-CQDs (Fig. 8E) and O-CQDs (Fig. 8F) as fluorescent probes, the fluorescence intensity increases steadily as increasing the concentration of P-123, the slope change of the fitted lines indicates the CMC of 0.30 mM and 0.25 mM, respectively. These CMC values by the CQDs as fluorescent probes at 20 °C are slightly lower than the literature data [69]. Although the surfactant P-123 is also a nonionic surfactant, it is found that the fluorescence intensity change in the presence of P-123 (Fig. 8D-F) is not similar to that in the presence of Triton X-100 (Fig. 6D-F), it should be ascribed to the different structure of the two surfactants. As comparing these two surfactants, the special phenyl group located between hydrophilic and hydrophobic parts in Triton X-100 should have a significant impact on the fluorescence properties of the CQDs, and the different chemical structures of the hydrophobic chains, alkyl hydrocarbon in Triton X-100 and poly(propyl glycol) in P-123, can also affect the fluorescence properties of the CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CMC determination by Stokes shift variations
ce:section.ce:para: Stokes shift, which is expressed as the wavelength distance between the emission maxima and excitation maxima, is an important property of the fluorophores [70]. The polarity change of the environment around the CQDs can be reflected by both emission maxima and excitation maxima, and thus results in a variation of the Stokes shift. Therefore, the variation of the Stokes shift as a function of surfactant concentration can be employed to deduce the CMC [66].

Fig. 9 shows the Stokes shift variation of the mixtures of the CQDs and different concentrations of CTAB, SDS or Triton X-100. The variation of the Stokes shift implies the micellization process of the surfactant, and finally the stable Stokes shift reflects the formation of micelles. So, the transition point of the Stokes shift corresponds to the CMC of the surfactants. As shown in Fig. 9A-C, the Stokes shift reduces as increasing the concentration of CTAB, the deduced CMC values by the G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and O-CQDs are 0.88 mM, 1.00 mM and 0.76 mM, respectively. The CMC values obtained from Stokes shift variations for CTAB are basically the same to those from the fluorescence intensity changes and are in good consistency with the literature [34]. Fig. 9D-F shows the relationship of the Stokes shift of the CQDs and different concentrations of SDS. With increasing the concentration of SDS in the micelle formation, the Stokes shift increases when the G-CQDs are used as fluorescent probes, but it decreases when the Y-CQDs and O-CQDs are used as fluorescent probes. This phenomenon further verifies the different interactions between the surfactant SDS and the three samples of the CQDs, and the transition points of 7.98 mM (Fig. 9D), 7.83 mM (Fig. 9E) and 8.01 mM (Fig. 9F) can be deduced as the CMC, which are slightly larger than those from the fluorescence intensity data and basically consistent with those in the literature [70]. For Triton X-100, the Stokes shift for the G-CQDs as fluorescent probes increase in the micelle formation (Fig. 9G), indicating a CMC value of 0.25 mM. However, for the Y-CQDs and O-CQDs as fluorescent probes, the Stokes shift reduces in the micellization (Fig. 9H and 9I), and the variations indicate the CMC values of 0.25 mM and 0.20 mM, respectively. The CMC values of Triton X-100 obtained by Stokes shift variation are consistent with the values from the fluorescence intensity changes and agree well with the data in the literature [28].

Additionally, the Stokes shift method was used to determine the CMC of OBS and P-123. Fig. 10A-C shows the relationship between the Stokes shift and the concentration of OBS. The Stokes shift for the G-CQDs and Y-CQDs as fluorescent probes grows in the micellization, while it decreases for the O-CQDs. As a result, the values of 1.02 mM, 1.00 mM and 1.00 mM can be signed as the CMC, which are highly consistent with the values obtained from the fluorescence intensity changes. The Stokes shift data versus the concentration of P-123 are shown in Fig. 10D-F. It is obvious that the Stokes shift gradually reduces in the micelle formation process of P-123. The deduced CMC values are 0.30 mM, 0.28 mM and 0.29 mM by the G-CQDs, Y-CQDs and O-CQDs, respectively. The CMC values are near to those from the fluorescence intensity data and those in the literature [69]. It is also found that the variation of Stokes shift in the micellization of different surfactants has a different tendency, which might further demonstrate the complicated interaction between the CQDs and the surfactants in the micellization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparative analysis of the CMC data
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of comparison, the CMC data deduced from the fluorescence intensity changes and the Stokes shift variations are shown in Table 1, as well as the published data in the references. For the cationic CTAB, the CMC values deduced from the fluorescence intensity changes are 0.97, 0.74 and 0.98 mM, while those from the Stokes shift variations are 0.88, 1.00 and 0.76 mM, respectively. Although there are slight differences in these data, they have good consistency with the literatures [22,34,59,66]. For the CMC of anionic SDS, the CMC data from the fluorescence intensity changes are 6.13, 6.88 and 7.70 mM, while those from the Stokes shift variations are 7.98, 7.83, and 8.01 mM; these data fall in the range of the published data [29,34,35,67]. The CMC values of nonionic surfactant Triton X-100 by the three samples of the CQDs from the fluorescence intensity data or the Stokes shift data are 0.24, 0.22, and 0.23 mM or 0.25, 0.25, and 0.20 mM, respectively; they are consistent with the literature values [28,34,67]. For the perfluoro surfactant OBS, the CMC values determined by the fluorescence intensity method are 0.89, 0.95, and 0.80 mM, while those by the Stokes shift method are 1.02, 1.00, and 1.00 mM; these values are near the published value of an isomer of OBS [68]. The CMC values of the triblock copolymer P-123 obtained from the fluorescence intensity changes are 0.24, 0.30 and 0.25 mM, while the values from the Stokes shift variations are 0.30, 0.28 and 0.29 mM, which are near to the value in the literature [69]. It is found that there is a slight difference in the CMC values of a same surfactant with three types of CQDs, this should be ascribed to the different interaction between the CQDs and the surfactant micelles which is related to the surface state of the CQDs, including the charge and the functional groups. If a type of CQDs has a strong interaction with the micelles, it may show a fluorescence response at the beginning of the micelle formation. Otherwise, if another type of CQDs has a relatively weak interaction with the micelles, there may be a delayed response after the complete formation of the micelles.

In summary, three samples of the fluorescent CQDs were synthesized by microwave hydrothermal carbonization and were directly employed as fluorescent probes to determine the CMC of representative surfactants via the data analysis of the fluorescence intensity and the Stokes shift. The obtained CMC values of CTAB, SDS, Triton X-100, perfluoro OBS, and triblock copolymer P-123 are consistent with the data in the published literature. The tendency of the fluorescence intensity changes and the Stokes shift variations are different for the three samples of the CQDs, demonstrating a complicated interaction between the CQDs and the surfactants in the micellization. The CQDs are demonstrated to be located at the palisade layer in the micelles due to the surface-attached hydrophilic groups though the CQDs are mainly hydrophobic as fluorescence probes. The fluorescence emission properties of the three samples of the CQDs are related to the ionic head type, the special group between hydrophilic and hydrophobic segments, and also the chemical nature of the hydrophobic chains of the surfactants, as well as the surface characteristics of the CQDs. The CQDs as fluorescent probes can be concluded as critical candidates for organic fluorescent dyes in the measurements of the CMC of surfactants and thus have much potential in the applications of the CMC measurement.

Dongxiang Li: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Xing Qian: Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Changshuo Lian: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software. Rui Huang: Data curation, Software, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Ziyi Hao: Data curation, Investigation, Software. Ming Cheng: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software. Shuping Li: Validation, Resources, Formal analysis. Chunfang Li: Project administration, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Shahid Iqbal: Formal analysis, Writing – review & editing. Lianjie Qin: Writing – review & editing. Jikuan Zhao: Conceptualization, Supervision, Methodology, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NNSFC 21975139), and the Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (ZR2017MB042), China.
